question: Define an isolated vertex in graph theory and state its degree.
expectedPoints: An isolated vertex is a vertex with no incident edges.
It is not connected to any other vertex in the graph.
The degree of an isolated vertex is 0.

question: Explain the difference between a finite graph and an infinite graph. Provide a simple example of a context where an infinite graph would be an appropriate model.
expectedPoints: A finite graph has a finite number of vertices and edges.
An infinite graph has an infinite number of vertices, edges, or both.
An appropriate example is modeling a computer network that is constantly expanding with new users and connections, where the graph's size is unbounded over time.

question: Prove that a graph is bipartite if and only if it contains no cycles of odd length. In your answer, clearly outline the two directions of the proof and use the concept of vertex degree and connectivity where appropriate.
expectedPoints: (=>) Direction: Assume the graph is bipartite. Show, by contradiction or construction, that any walk starting and ending in the same partite set must have even length, thus no odd cycle can exist.

(<=) Direction: Assume the graph has no odd cycles. Describe the process of using a vertex's connectivity and distance from a starting vertex to partition the graph into two sets, proving this partition is valid because an edge between two vertices of the same set would create a cycle of odd length.
Emphasize the role of graph connectivity; if the graph is disconnected, the process is applied to each connected component separately.
